records.ety: [OE. scorchen, probably akin to scorcnen; cf. Norw. skrokken shrunk up, skrekka, skrökka, to shrink, to become wrinkled up, dial. Sw. skrkkla to wrinkle (see Shrug); but perhaps influenced by OF. escorchier to strip the bark from, to flay, to skin, F. corcher, LL. excorticare; L. ex from + cortex, -icis, bark (cf. Cork); because the skin falls off when scorched.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Scorched (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Scorching.]

defs: To burn superficially; to parch, or shrivel, the surface of, by heat; to subject to so much heat as changes color and texture without consuming; as, to scorch linen.
quotes: Summer drouth or singd air Never scorch thy tresses fair.

defs: To affect painfully with heat, or as with heat; to dry up with heat; to affect as by heat.
quotes: Lashed by mad rage, and scorched by brutal fires.

defs: To burn; to destroy by, or as by, fire.
quotes: Power was given unto him to scorch men with fire.
The fire that scorches me to death.

defs: To be burnt on the surface; to be parched; to be dried up.
quotes: Scatter a little mungy straw or fern amongst your seedlings, to prevent the roots from scorching.
He laid his long forefinger on the scarlet letter, which forthwith seemed to scorch into Hester's breast, as if it had been red hot.

defs: To ride or drive at great, usually at excessive, speed; -- applied chiefly to automobilists and bicyclists. [Colloq.]
